children.name: Anguis
children.description: Slow worms — legless lizards of Europe. The Slow Worm is one of the most familiar Swedish reptiles — a smooth copper-brown legless lizard found in gardens, allotments, and woodland edges across most of the country. It eats slugs and earthworms, making it a welcome garden guest. Legless lizards differ from snakes in having eyelids, external ear openings, and the ability to shed the tail.

children.name: Anguis fragilis
children.description: A legless lizard, not a snake; common in Swedish gardens and forest edges; eats slugs and earthworms; copper-brown with metallic sheen; Swedish name means 'copper snake'; can shed and partially regrow its tail; lives 20-30 years in the wild.

children.name: Anguis colchica
children.description: Recently split from A. fragilis; eastern distribution; virtually identical in appearance.

children.name: Pseudopus apodus
children.description: Large legless lizard reaching 1.4 m; vestigial hind limb stubs visible near cloaca; crushes molluscs and beetles with powerful jaws; Balkan grasslands and steppe.

children.name: Ophisaurus ventralis
children.description: Up to 1.1 m; breaks into pieces when grabbed, giving the group its name; can regenerate the tail only.

children.name: Elgaria multicarinata
children.description: Rough overlapping scales suggest armour plating; agile climber; defensive biting.
